title: Pilosella serpyllifolia
intro: Pilosella serpyllifolia, also known as mouse-ear hawk's-beard, is a perennial plant species belonging to the sunflower family (Asteraceae). The plant has been used in traditional medicine for its potential health benefits, including anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antimicrobial properties, which may help to alleviate symptoms of various conditions such as digestive issues and wounds. The bioactive constituents of Pilosella serpyllifolia include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and sesquiterpene lactones, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made with Pilosella serpyllifolia include teas, tinctures, and infusions, which are often used to support digestive health and immune function.
taxonomy: Pilosella serpyllifolia is a member of the dandelion family, Asteraceae. It belongs to the genus Pilosella, which is a group of flowering plants often referred to as "false dandelions." This plant is a type of apomictic species, meaning it reproduces without fertilization and produces seeds that are genetically identical to the parent plant. Pilosella serpyllifolia is native to Europe and parts of Asia, and is often found growing in meadows, fields, and along roadsides. Its classification is further detailed at the species level, with Pilosella serpyllifolia being a distinct species within the Pilosella genus.

side_effects: Pilosella serpyllifolia, also known as mouse-ear hawk's-beard or false mouse-ear, is a plant species native to Europe, Western Asia, and North Africa. The plant is considered toxic and its consumption can lead to gastrointestinal symptoms, such as nausea and vomiting, in humans. Ingestion of the plant's leaves and flowers can also cause liver damage and other systemic effects. The exact mechanisms behind these toxic effects are not fully understood, but they are thought to be caused by the plant's glycosides. As with any potentially toxic plant, it's essential to exercise caution when handling or consuming Pilosella serpyllifolia.
